coredata.dc:title: A novel thiol-ene click reaction for preparation of graphene quantum dots and their potential for fluorescence imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Hongye
Liu, Meiying
Tuo, Xun
Chen, Junyu
Mao, Liucheng
Wen, Yuanqing
Tian, Jianwen
Zhou, Naigen
Zhang, Xiaoyong
Wei, Yen
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a kind of carbon dots with the structure of graphene, possess fascinating properties of both carbon dots and graphene have attracted increasing attention for various applications especially in the biomedical fields. It is therefore, many methods for preparation of GQDs have been developed over the last decade. However, most of the previous reports are required tedious experimental procedure and hazardous agents. In this study, we developed an unparalleled preparation method for preparation of GQDs from graphene oxide through a one-step thiol-ene click reaction. More importantly, many carboxyl groups have been introduced on the surface of GQDs during this procedure. The characterization results demonstrated that these GQDs display small size, uniform morphology, high water dispersibility and strong green fluorescence. Biological assays suggested that these GQDs are of low cytotoxicity and efficient cell uptake performance. More importantly, many other GQDs could also been fabricated through the thiol-ene click reaction owing to the universality of thiol-ene click reaction. Therefore, this novel strategy based on thiol-ene click reaction should be of great importance for advancing the preparation and biomedical applications of GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Thiol-ene click reaction
Biological imaging
Fluorescent nanoprobes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GQDs via thiol-ene click reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Normalized FT-IR spectra of GO and GQDs.
XPS spectra of GO and GQDs. (A) Survey scan ranging from 0 to 1200 eV, (B) S2p region, (C) C1s spectra of GQ, (D) C1s spectra of GQDs.
Representative TEM images of pristine GO (A) and GQDs (B).
(A) fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of GQDs. (B) UV–Vis spectrum of GQDs, Inset: Photographs taken without (right cuvette) and with (left cuvette) UV right at 365 nm.
Fluorescence emission spectra (A) and Fluorescence intensity value vs time (B) of GQDs (500 μg mL−1) under irradiating by UV lamp (365 nm) for 3 h.
Cell viability evaluation of GQDs based on the CCK-8 assay. Cells were incubated with different concentrations (10–120 μg mL−1) of for 8 and 24 h.
Cell imaging of GQDs. fluorescent images (A), bright field (B), the overlap image of A and B (C), scale bar = 20 μm. The cells were excited by a laser with wavelength of 405 nm.
Schematic showing the preparation of the GQDs which relied on the thiol-ene click reaction between graphene oxide and mercaptosuccinic acid.
Element content (%) of GO and GQDs were calculated based on the XPS analysis.

head.ce:title: A novel thiol-ene click reaction for preparation of graphene quantum dots and their potential for fluorescence imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang University, Nanchang, Jiangxi 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Center for Nanotechnology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a kind of carbon dots with the structure of graphene, possess fascinating properties of both carbon dots and graphene have attracted increasing attention for various applications especially in the biomedical fields. It is therefore, many methods for preparation of GQDs have been developed over the last decade. However, most of the previous reports are required tedious experimental procedure and hazardous agents. In this study, we developed an unparalleled preparation method for preparation of GQDs from graphene oxide through a one-step thiol-ene click reaction. More importantly, many carboxyl groups have been introduced on the surface of GQDs during this procedure. The characterization results demonstrated that these GQDs display small size, uniform morphology, high water dispersibility and strong green fluorescence. Biological assays suggested that these GQDs are of low cytotoxicity and efficient cell uptake performance. More importantly, many other GQDs could also been fabricated through the thiol-ene click reaction owing to the universality of thiol-ene click reaction. Therefore, this novel strategy based on thiol-ene click reaction should be of great importance for advancing the preparation and biomedical applications of GQDs.

A novel one-step thiol-ene click reaction has been developed for preparation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The biological applications of these GQDs were also investigated.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of nanotechnology, luminescent nanomaterials have become the hotspots in both basic research and practical applications [1,2]. Especially in the biomedical fields, luminescent nanomaterials could play a pivotal role in biosensors, biological imaging, drug delivery and theranostics [1,3–6]. Furthermore, more and more luminescent nanomaterials have been successfully developed for biomedical applications, such as semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [7], luminescent silicon nanoparticles [8], fluorescent organic nanoparticles [5,6,9] and metallic nanoclusters [10]. Thereinto, QDs were the most promising building blocks for luminescent nanomaterials used in biomedical applications, especially for their stable photostability and ultrasmall size [8,11,12]. However, the traditional QDs were always impeded by their inherent toxicity, which restrict their further biomedical applications [13]. Fortunately, compared with conventional QDs, the carbon dots possessed low toxicity, well water dispersible and excellent biocompatibility [14,15]. For example, the work of Sun and Yang et al. has reported that the carbon dots are nontoxic effects for the mice [14]. Besides, they further demonstrated the successful optical imaging in mice via carbon dots [16]. With the tremendous research interest of carbon dots, a variety of methods have been developed to prepare carbon dots, including natural gas, electrochemical oxidation of graphite, electrochemical soaking of carbon nanotubes and so on [17–20].

Among the various kinds of carbon dots, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have gradually become the most promising alternative candidates for biomedical applications [21]. It could be ascribed to the common properties of carbon dots, GQDs could also possess other excellent properties from graphene. Nowadays, graphene as a kind of single atom thick layer of carbon nanomaterials, has attracted extensive attentions due to its superior mechanical flexibility, high efficiency migration of electrons, specific surface area and remarkable thermal properties [22–25]. Nevertheless, no any fluorescence could be detected in the pristine graphene, which is resulted from that graphene is a zero-bandgap semiconductor and lacks a bandgap [26–28]. Fortunately, it could transform the 2D graphene sheets into 0D GQDs through fragmentation of graphene sheets. The GQDs have a non-zero bandgap and luminescence upon excitation, which could be attributed to the quantum confinement and edge effect [29–31]. Therefore, the GQDs could possess photoluminescence, nanoscale particle size, chemical stability, nontoxicity and other excellent properties from graphene. Furthermore, it is noteworthy that many carboxylic groups exist at the edge of the most GQDs, which is from the raw material GO [21]. On the hand, a number of carboxyl moieties could improve their water solubility and biocompatibility. On the other hand, we could utilize the carboxylic groups to modify the surface of GQDs and even load with some drug molecules. In view to the emerging properties of GQDs, many preparation methods of GQDs have been developed, such as electrochemical synthesis [32], ultrasonic shearing [33], microwave-assisted cutting [34] and long-time chemical reaction [35]. Whereas, conventional preparation methods require the harsh reaction conditions but get low yield, which limits their mass production. Hence, the searching of novel and high efficiency preparation methods should be of great importance for the development of GQDs [36,37].

Thiol-ene reaction is the simple hydrothiolation of carbon‑carbon double bond, which has been known for over 100 years [38]. Recently, thiol-ene chemistry has been regarded as a new type of click chemistry, which could offer many advantages, including high regioselectivity, mild reaction conditions and high conversion [39–42]. Furthermore, it has been wildly reported to functionalize carbon nanomaterials via thiol-ene click chemistry [43,44]. For example, Zabihi and Naebe have demonstrated the surface modification of multiwall carbon nanotubes by thiol-ene click chemistry [45]. Meanwhile, it should exist a number of CC bonds in graphene networks, which could react with the thiol groups containing compounds through the thiol-ene click reaction.

In this contribution, a facile strategy has been developed for the first time for preparation of GQDs through the thiol-ene click reaction. As shown in Scheme 1 , we prepared the graphene oxide (GO) via a modified Hummers' method [46]. Then the mercaptosuccinic acid was introduced on GO through thiol-ene click reaction to generate GQDs [9]. It is noticeable that the mercaptosuccinic acid could bring more carboxyl groups to GQDs, which could improve their water solubility and for further conjugation with other functional components or adsorption of drugs. To explore their biomedical application potential, the physicochemical properties (such as size, water dispersibility, functional groups and fluorescence), cell viability and cell uptake behavior of GQDs were further examined.

All chemical were of analytical grade and were used as received without any further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with doubly distilled water. Purified natural graphene powders were procured from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co, Ltd. All other chemicals such as:mercaptosuccinic acid (Mw: 150.15 Da, 98%) and Dicumyl peroxyide (Mw: 270.37 Da, 99%) were also purchased from Sinopharm Chemical reagents Co, Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a Hitachi 7650B microscope operated at 80 kV; the TEM samples were made by placing a drop of nanoparticle ethanol suspension on a carbon-coated copper grid. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained by using a Nicolet 380 Fourier transform spectrometer with a resolution of 2 cm−1. The samples were pressed with KBr into a pellet before measuring the infrared absorption spectra. 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrum was recorded on Bruker Avance-400 spectrometer with DMSO as the solvent. The fluorescence data were obtained from the Fluorescence spectrophotometer (FSP, model: C11367-11), which was purchased from Hamamatsu (Japan). The reported values are the mean values of the three measurements. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were performed on a VGESCALAB 220-IXL spectrometer using an Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV). MWCO of the dialysis membrane is 3500 Da. The UV–Vis spectrum was obtained from an ultraviolet and visible spectrophotometer (TU 1810 pc, Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co.)

We utilize graphite powders as raw materials to prepare the single-layer GO sheets via modified Hummers' method [46]. A mixture of graphite powder (1 g), NaNO3 (0.5 g), KMnO4 (6 g) and H2SO4 (46 mL) was poured into a 500 mL conical flask kept in an ice-water bath. After the mixture stirred at ambient temperature for 15 min, deionized water (92 mL) was added into the flack dropwisely. Afterward, the conical flask was placed in an oil bath and stirred at 98 °C for 15 min. Then, 140 mL of water and 3 mL of H2O2 (30%) were added under ultrasonic irradiation for 30 min. Finally the mixture was centrifuged and washed with HCl solution (3%) for three times. The resulting product could be obtained by dialysis with deionized water for three days to remove the acid and other ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs via thiol-ene click reaction
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic GO (100 mg) was dispersed in anhydrous DMF. After ultrasonic treatment for 10 min, 1 g of mercaptosuccinic acid and 1.2 g of dicumyl peroxyide were added into GO suspensions. Then, the mixture was further stirred at 150 °C for 24 h under reflux condition and continuous nitrogen atmosphere. The supernatant liquid in mixture could be obtained by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min. Finally the supernatant liquor was dialyzed against distilled water for 3 days to remove any remaining by-product and reactant. And we could obtain the final product via rotary evaporation. (The production yield is about 67%).

The cell viability is an important parameter for evaluation of biocompatibility of biomaterials in various biomedical fields. Therefore, the cell viability values of GQDs were determined using a well-known assay named as cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) using L929 cells [47–50]. L929 cells were seeded in 96-well microplates at a density of 5 × 104 cells per mL in 160 μL of the respective media containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). After 24 h of cell attachment, L929 cells were incubated with 10–120 μg mL−1 of GQDs for 8 and 24 h. Then the cells were washed with PBS for three times to remove the uninternalized nanoparticles. After that, 10 μL of CCK-8 dye and 100 μL of Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) cell culture media was added to each well and incubated for 2 h at 37 °C. Plates were then analyzed with a microplate reader (VictorIII, Perkin-Elmer). Measurements of dye absorbance were carried out at 450 nm, with the reference wavelength at 620 nm. The values were proportional to the number of live cells. The percent reduction of CCK-8 was compared to the control (cells not exposed to nanoparticles), which represented 100% CCK-8 reduction. Three replicate wells were used for each control and test concentrations per microplate, and the experiment was repeated three times. Cell survival was expressed as absorbance relative to that of untreated controls. Results are presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD).

L929 cells were facilely bred in Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM), which was supplied with 10% heat-inactivated FBS, 2 mM glutamine, 100 U mL−1 penicillin, and 100 μg mL−1 of streptomycin. Before proceeding experiment of cell imaging, the cell culture should be controlled at 37 °C in a similar human environment of 95% air and 5% CO2 in culture medium. In order to maintain the exponential growth of the cells, the culture medium should be updated every three days. Before treatment, cells were seeded in a glass bottom dish with a density of 1 × 105 cells per dish. On the day of treatment, the cells were incubated with GQDs at a final concentration of 30 μg mL−1 for 3 h at 37 °C. Afterward, the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove the GQDs and then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10 min at room temperature. Cell images were obtained using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) Zesis 710 3-channel (Zesis, Germany) with the excitation wavelength of 405 nm.

GQDs, which are a kind of carbon dots, have attracted enormous research attention due to its long term resistance to photobleaching, high efficiency migration of electrons, remarkable thermal properties, low cytotoxicity, and ultrasmall size. Furthermore, the GQDs possessed not only the common advantages of most carbon dots but also other remarkable properties from the graphene. Generally speaking, the preparation methods of GQDs could be divided into top-down and bottom-up methods. Here, we developed a novel top-down method based on a simple thiol-ene click reaction. Compared with the traditional preparation methods, this method only requires one-step simple chemical reaction. More importantly, the synthetic GO and mercaptosuccinic acid would make the GQDs possessed a number of carboxyl groups on their surface, which would endow them with excellent dispersibility in aqueous solution and further surface functionalization. The successful formation and remarkable properties of GQDs would be exhibited by a series of characterization techniques. 1H NMR spectrum of GQDs was displayed in Fig. S1. A series of peaks around 2.4–3.3 ppm could be observed. It can be attributed to the methylene from the mercaptosuccinic acid on the surface of GQDs. Nevertheless, we could not observe the chemical shift signals of carboxyl in the 1H NMR spectrum of GQDs, because carboxyl groups are the free functional groups.

The FT-IR spectroscopy was a powerful evidence to prove the successful synthesis of GQDs via the thiol-ene click reaction. The FT-IR spectra of GO and GQDs were shown Fig. 1 . It was found that there are a series of characteristic absorption peaks in the spectra of GO and GQDs. They respectively located at 3500, 2900, 1690 and 1100 cm−1, which were corresponding to the OH, CH2 , CO and O. Compared with GO, it can be seen that the peak intensities of CH2 and CO were significantly enhanced in GQDs. The obvious peaks of CH2 suggested that mercaptosuccinic acid has introduced on the surface of GQDs. On the other hand, the stretching vibrations of CO belonging to the carboxyl groups were also detected from the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs. This further indicated that mercaptosuccinic acid has successfully immobilized on GQDs through the thiol-ene click reaction. Therefore, based on the FT-IR results, we could conclude that GQDs with many carboxyl groups have been successfully fabricated via the thiol-ene click reaction.

Expect from FT-IR spectra, the XPS could give us other evidences for the successful fabrication of GQDs, via following the chemical composition variation of the reactants during the thiol-ene click reaction. As seen in Fig. 2A, we could observe that there are the elements, including carbon (286 eV), oxygen (543 eV), nitrogen (400 eV) and sulfur (166 eV) in the GO and GQDs. The appearance of element sulfur clearly indicated that mercaptosuccinic acid has introduced in GQDs successfully. The detailed XPS spectra of sulfur, oxygen and nitrogen were shown in Fig. 2B and Fig. S2. It should be noted that the S 2p intensity of GQDs was significantly enhanced compared with the GO, which come from mercaptosuccinic acid and confirmed the successful thiol-ene click chemical reaction. Furthermore, Fig. 2C and D depicts the XPS spectra of C 1s of the GO and GQDs. We could divide their C 1s signal peak into the four peaks located at 284.8, 286, 287.4 and 288.5 eV, which could be attributed to the CC/CC, CO, CO, and COOH bonds respectively [51]. It was evident that the peaks of COOH remarkably strengthen in GQDs, which were from mercaptosuccinic acid. Therefore, the results of XPS spectra could further indicate that successful preparation of GQDs by the thiol-ene click reaction.

Based on the XPS analysis, the content of different elements in GO and GQDs is exhibited in Table 1 . The overall atomic percentage of elements present in GO was carbon (68.94%), oxygen (28.99%), sulfur (0.62%) and nitrogen (1.45%). After the thiol-ene click reaction, the element percentages of GQDs were changed to 76.54%, 17.07%, 4.19% and 2.20% for C, O, S and N respectively. On the one hand, the increase of carbon contents and decrease of oxygen contents implied that the GO should be carbonized in some degree during the high-temperature reaction [52]. On the other hand, the content of sulfur elements was noticeable improved, on account of the surface modification of GQDs by mercaptosuccinic acid. Hence, the comparison of the element contents of GO and GQDs could further suggest the successful synthesis of GQDs.

The morphology and size of GO and GQDs could be clearly demonstrated by TEM images. As shown in Fig. 3A, the TEM image of GO looks like a paper-like morphology, which are consistent with 2D hexagonal lattice structure of GO. This could convincingly confirm the successful preparation of GO from the graphite powders via a modified Hummers' method. Furthermore, it should be noted that the stratified structure of GO is distinct thin, which further suggest the GO sheets with large surface areas. On the other hand, the TEM image of GQDs is shown in the Fig. 3B. It is obvious that the collected GQDs are homodisperse and have a uniform diameter about 2–10 nm. The small size may be the possible mechanism for the fluorescence of GQDs. More significantly, these GQDs with ultrasmall size were suitable to penetrate through cell membranes for biological imaging. In addition, the hydrodynamic size distribution of GQDs could be further measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS), which is shown in Fig. S4. The hydrodynamic size of GQDs is 6.96 ± 3.51 nm and the polydispersity index (PDI) is only 0.219. Hence, we could observe the detailed morphology and size of GO and GQDs from TEM images, which could reveal the potential biological imaging application of GQDs.

The excellent fluorescence property of GQDs would be further recorded by fluorescence spectra, which is exhibited in Fig. 4A. The maximum emission wavelength was located at 497 nm, while the optimal excitation wavelength was appeared about 400 nm. The multitudinous emission wavelengths of synthetic GQDs in aqueous solution have been demonstrated at different excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. S3, it was obvious that the fluorescence emission peaks of GQDs shift to longer wavelength from 425 to 525 nm with the excitation wavelength ranges from 330 to 450 nm. These results could imply that the fluorescence properties of GQDs might be depended on grain diameter. This phenomenon is also observed from other carbon dots in previous reports [53,54]. On the other hand, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of GQDs dispersed in water is shown in Fig. 4B. It can be seen that an adsorption peak at 235 nm could be observed in the spectrum, that can be attributed to the π → π* transition of CC and aromatic rings. Moreover, another the adsorption peak at 295 nm can be assigned to the n → π* transition which could be deduced from carboxyl groups. Furthermore, the wavelength of the absorption peak at 400 nm, which is almost the same as that of the excitation peak of GQDs. In addition, the inset (left cuvette) of Fig. 4B suggests the great dispersibility of GQDs in water, because the “GQDs” as background could be observed clearly in the inset (left cuvette). The zeta potential is another important factor to research the dispersibility of colloidal solution, which is displayed in Fig. S5. The results demonstrated that the value of zeta potential range from −48.8 to 21.1 mV and the absolute value of zeta potential reach to 13.9 mV, indicating the outstanding dispersibility of GQDs in aqueous solution. More interestingly, the insert (right cuvette) of Fig. 4B could further confirm that GQDs emit strong green fluorescence and disperse in pure water very well. According to the results of fluorescence and absorption spectra, it should be certain that the GQDs possess outstanding optical properties and well water dispersibility, which are expected to have high potential for biological imaging.

The excellent photostability of QDs has been extensively reported, which would provide the necessary condition for biological imaging application [55,56]. In particular, the GQDs could keep long-term resistance to photobleaching [57]. As displayed in Fig. 5 , the water suspension of GQDs with the same concentration was respectively irradiated by UV lamp (365 nm) for 0, 30, 60,120 and 180 min. It is evident that the original fluorescence intensity value is 250 a.u. After irradiation for 30 min, the fluorescence intensity was decreased to 245 a.u., which only loss the 2% fluorescence intensity. More importantly, even if the suspension was irradiated for 3 h, the fluorescence intensity could still keep 217 a.u. Therefore, these results demonstrate that the GQDs possess the distinguished photostability, which makes them pretty candidates for biological imaging.

The conventional QDs possess many great properties for various applications, like the stable photostability and small particle size. Nevertheless, obvious toxicity and poor biodegradability always limits their practical biomedical applications. Fortunately, most carbon dots are nontoxic for various cells. In this work, the preliminary biocompatibility of GQDs was evaluated using CCK-8 assay. The cell viability results of GQDs for L929 cells were displayed in Fig. 6 . It can be seen that hardly any cell viability changes could be observed after L929 cells were incubated with 10–120 μg mL−1 of GQDs for 8 and 24 h. More importantly, the cell viability values could still keep above 90%, when the concentration is even up to 120 μg mL−1. All of these above results suggested that GQDs are of low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility. This is very crucial for their applications in biomedical fields. Moreover, the GQDs could also be useful carrying the anticancer agent cisplatin owing to a large number of carboxyl groups on their surface. It has been demonstrated that the carboxyl groups could coordinate with cisplatin and release it with pH responsiveness. Therefore, the GQDs should also be potentially used for intracellular controlled drug delivery.

Based on all the aforementioned features, it could be concluded that we have successfully prepared the GQDs with bright green fluorescence, stable photostability, low cytotoxicity and uniform nanoscale size via the thiol-ene click reaction. Considered these outstanding properties, it is necessary to explore their potential in biological imaging. Thus, we further evaluate the cell uptake behavior about GQDs via CLSM observation (Fig. 7 ). Intense fluorescence signals could be observed in the location of cells, after the cells have been incubated with 30 μg mL−1 of GQDs for 3 h (Fig. 7A). On the other hand, many areas with weak fluorescence intensity were also found in the location of cells, which were surrounded by areas with strong fluorescence signals. Hence, we could speculate that the areas with weak or no fluorescence might be the locations of cell nuclei. These results indicate that the GQDs could be internalized by cells through non-specific endocytosis and mainly distributed in cytoplasm. More interestingly, it is obvious that the size and morphology of cells shows no any abnormal changes in Fig. 7B, which could further verify the excellent biocompatibility of GQDs. The merged image from fluorescence image and bright field image was displayed in Fig. 7C. We found that the fluorescence signals were just overlaid with the cell locations. This clearly evidenced that the GQDs were internalized by cells and distributed in the cytoplasm. Therefore, the efficient cell uptake of GQDs indicated their highly potential for biological imaging and imaging-guided therapy.

In conclusion, we have demonstrated a novel and facile method for preparation of GQDs from GO based on the one-step thiol-ene click reaction for the first time. Compared with traditional synthesis of GQDs, this preparation method through one-step click reaction is very cheap and efficient. Furthermore, we have also shown their outstanding properties, like uniform nanoscale size, strong green fluorescence, stable photostability and excellent biocompatibility. In consideration of these properties, we further explored the potential of GQDs in cell imaging. More interestingly, there are a number of carboxyl groups on the surface of GQDs, which inherit from GO and mercaptosuccinic acid. These carboxyl groups could impart the GQDs with great water solubility and suitability for subsequent modification with various polymers and for drug delivery. Finally, many other components might be also used for preparation of GQDs to fabricate GQDs with different functional groups and properties. Therefore, the high-efficiency synthetic strategy based on one-step thiol-ene click reaction is expected to be a universal tool for fabrication of GQDs with designable properties for various biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51363016, 21474057, 21564006, 21561022, 21644014), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province in China (Nos. 20161BAB203072, 20161BAB213066).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2018.05.025.
